corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

We demonstrate that large gains on these tasks can be realized by generative pre-training of a language model on a diverse corpus of unlabeled text, followed by discriminative fine-tuning on each specific task.
In contrast to previous approaches, we make use of task-aware input transformations during fine-tuning to achieve effective transfer while requiring minimal changes to the model architecture.

Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that use architectures specifically crafted for each task, significantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied.

Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that use architectures specifically crafted for each task, significantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied.
Existing techniques involve a combination of making task-specific changes to the model architecture , using intricate learning schemes and adding auxiliary learning objectives .
These uncertainties have made it difficult to develop effective semi-supervised learning approaches for language processing.
We assume access to a large corpus of unlabeled text and several datasets with manually annotated training examples (target tasks).
Our setup does not require these target tasks to be in the same domain as the unlabeled corpus.
For our model architecture, we use the Transformer , which has been shown to perform strongly on various tasks such as machine translation , document generation , and syntactic parsing .
As we demonstrate in our experiments, these adaptations enable us to fine-tune effectively with minimal changes to the architecture of the pre-trained model.

Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that employ architectures specifically crafted for each task, significantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied.

Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that employ architectures specifically crafted for each task, significantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied.

For instance, we achieve absolute improvements of 8.9% on commonsense reasoning (Stories Cloze Test) , 5.7% on question answering (RACE) , 1.5% on textual entailment (MultiNLI) and 5.5% on the recently introduced GLUE multi-task benchmark .
Over the last few years, researchers have demonstrated the benefits of using word embeddings , which are trained on unlabeled corpora, to improve performance on a variety of tasks .
Phrase-level or sentence-level embeddings, which can be trained using an unlabeled corpus, have been used to encode text into suitable vector representations for various target tasks .
This involves a substantial amount of new parameters for each separate target task, whereas we require minimal changes to our model architecture during transfer.
The first stage is learning a high-capacity language model on a large corpus of text.
We also observe the LSTM exhibits higher variance in its zero-shot performance suggesting that the inductive bias of the Transformer architecture assists in transfer.
